romania.title: România se remarcă în competiția globală HONOR Talents: NextGen Creators premiază noua generație de artiști și designeri la București

romania.title: Băluţă anunţă finalizarea primei etape la Planşeul Unirii: Zona Hanului lui Manuc intră în teste. Ciucu: lucrările sunt în grafic
romania.summary: Primarul Sectorului 4 Daniel Băluţă a anunţat, luni, că lucrările la prima etapă a refacerii Planşeului Unirii, în zona Hanului lui Manuc, au fost finalizate, iar sectorul va fi supus testelor realizate de specialiştii Universităţii Tehnice de Construcţii Bucureşti. și primarul Capitalei Ciprian Ciucu a declarat că investiţia este una de mare amploare şi a apreciat că lucrările sunt în graficul stabilit.

romania.title: 😂 Kelemen i-a adus lui Grindeanu desertul lui preferat: kürvős kalács
romania.summary: Un moment emoţionant a avut loc în această dimineaţă la consultările între partide! Ca să-şi arate disponibilitatea de a participa la orice fel de coaliţie, liderul UDMR Kelemen Hunor i-a dăruit şefului PSD Sorin Grindeanu desertul său preferat: un kürvős kalács. „Nimic nu e mai reprezentativ pentru spiritul UDMR decât un kürvős kalács proaspăt, cald, […] The post Kelemen i-a adus lui Grindeanu desertul lui preferat: kürvős kalács appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: FLASH SALE: 70% reducere la biletele pentru copii la Wonder Family Fest 2026, doar de Ziua Familiei (15 mai)
romania.summary: De Ziua Familiei, sărbătorită pe data de 15 mai, organizatorii celui mai mare festival pentru familii din România vin cu un cadou special: biletele și abonamentele de acces destinate copiilor au 70% reducere*, în ediție limitată de 3000 bucăți. Prețurile pornesc de la 147 de lei pentru biletele de o zi și 297 de lei pentru abonamentele cu acces general (pentru toate cele trei zile de festival).

romania.summary: Simpatizanții lui Călin Georgescu râd de cei care neagă că gurul lor ar fi donat 4 milioane de dolari din banii personali orfanilor din România. Argumentele aduse de aceștia sunt considerate de-a dreptul ridicole. „Cum poți să spui că Domnul Georgescu nu avea acei bani, cât de ticălos să fii să susții așa ceva?”, a […] The post Fanii lui Georgescu știu de unde are acesta milioane pentru donații: din salariul de la Real Madrid appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: După ce şi-au încântat fanii din Atena cu un fragment din „Zorba”, americanii de la Metallica sunt hotărâţi să se autodepăşească la concertul de pe Arena Naţională din Bucureşti! Aceştia au studiat ore întregi de înregistrări de la nunţi pentru a găsi un echivalent românesc pentru Zorba, şi se pare că l-au găsit: îndrăgitul Dans […] The post O să fie WOW! După Zorba la greci, Metallica învaţă Dansul Pinguinului pentru concertul din România appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Explicații pentru saltul „ratat” cu parașuta. Greșeala făcută de sportivul care a sărit pentru prima dată de la 1.200 de metri
romania.summary: Imagini cu ceea ce pare a fi un salt ratat cu parașuta și o aterizare norocoasă au creat senzație pe internet. Incidentul a avut loc în apropiere de Craiova, iar protagonistul este un elev al aeroclubului local. Acesta a avut probleme cu parașuta principală și a deschis-o pe cea de rezervă. Aterizarea a fost făcută în siguranță, a spus la Digi24 directorul general al Aeroclubului României, George Rotaru.

În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: Dietary changes in older people can improve 'biological age'
medical.summary: Older Australians who reduce either dietary fat or animal-based protein show signs of reduced biological age, new research from the University of Sydney shows. Published in Aging Cell, the findings revealed 65- to 75-year-olds subjected to dietary intervention for just four weeks showed a reduction in "biological age" based on their biomarker profile.

medical.title: Annual frailty screening recommended for the over 65s
medical.summary: A new framework to prevent and manage frailty in older Australians has been developed in a national project led by University of Queensland researchers. The Australian Consensus Statements are recommendations designed in consultation with health care professionals, older adults with lived experience of frailty, and caregivers across the country.

medical.title: A common cholesterol drug may weaken ovarian cancer's hidden shield
medical.summary: Ascites—the buildup of liquid in the belly—may be doing more than causing discomfort. A Duke University School of Medicine study finds this fluid helps cancer cells survive and spread—and that a decades-old cholesterol drug may be able to disrupt that protection.

medical.title: Depression may be overdiagnosed in patients with ovarian cancer
medical.summary: People with ovarian cancer could be being overdiagnosed with depression due to the physical symptoms they experience as part of the cancer, according to international researchers. In addition to causing mental symptoms such as sadness and despair, depression can cause physical sensations including fatigue, headaches, back pain, gastrointestinal issues, and sleep problems.

medical.title: Brain-controlled hearing system isolates one speaker in noisy settings, first human tests show
medical.summary: Scientists at Columbia University's Zuckerman Institute have the first direct evidence from human studies that brain-controlled hearing technology can help people single out a voice in a crowd. These early findings suggest that researchers may one day develop a hearing augmentation device that can, among other feats, overcome the problems that conventional hearing aids have with noisy surroundings.

medical.title: Urine test could flag bipolar, ADHD and anorexia years earlier
medical.summary: New research suggests a simple urine test could help spot conditions including bipolar disorder, ADHD and anorexia much sooner, easing pressure on health services where diagnoses can currently take months—even years.

medical.title: In older adults, adding whey protein doesn't make more muscle
medical.summary: Packing in the protein is all the rage. From cereal to pasta to nacho chips and more, food manufacturers are trying to get as much of the stuff into their products as possible, and one of the benefits they tout is that protein increases muscle mass and strength.

medical.title: Q&A: Expert explains why tick bites are sending people to the ER in droves
medical.summary: Early-season tick bites have been driving more people to emergency rooms since 2017, and Virginia, along with several other northeastern states, is in the bull's-eye.

medical.title: Attention to sex differences, relapse needed in early-stage studies of medication to treat alcohol use disorder
medical.summary: Early-stage studies of medications to treat high-risk alcohol use may be missing opportunities to identify potential therapeutic effects in certain populations, including women and people at risk for relapse, according to a systematic review published in Alcohol: Clinical & Experimental Research.

medical.title: Slow-dividing breast cancer cells may explain relapses decades after treatment
medical.summary: A new study by the Garvan Institute of Medical Research has uncovered a hidden mechanism explaining why breast cancer can return many years after successful treatment. Published in Nature Communications, the research reveals rogue cells that change their programming to allow them to divide at a remarkably slow pace, meaning they could form microscopic tumors that silently tick away in distant organs, evading detection for decades.

medical.title: Improving relationship quality may support recovery from alcohol use disorder
medical.summary: For people trying to reduce their heavy drinking, the quality of their interpersonal relationships early in their recovery process may contribute to their success. A study published in Alcohol: Clinical & Experimental Research found that those who were successful in achieving remission from alcohol use disorder had reduced negative interactions with their family and social network in the first month after they began their recovery process. The study suggests that training family and friends how to positively engage with people trying to reduce their drinking may improve their chances of success.

medical.title: Alcohol use disorder and obesity may cause metabolic alterations that raise the risk of relapse, study suggests
medical.summary: Disrupted metabolic function in people with alcohol use disorder (AUD) may be amplified in the context of obesity, contributing to intensified alcohol cravings, according to a preliminary study that supports ongoing investigation of GLP-1 agonists as a potential AUD treatment.

medical.title: Laser-induced acoustic imaging maps hidden nerves and vessels during robot surgery
medical.summary: Surgery is a complicated endeavor. Even a successful surgery can lead to complications, and even the best surgeons sometimes have unsuccessful surgeries. A surgeon must rely on visual cues and their own experience to avoid hitting a nerve or a blood vessel, mistakes that can turn a simple surgery into a much more challenging one.

medical.title: The robotic penguin that makes endoscopy optional
medical.summary: Researchers at the TechMed Center of the University of Twente have built a swallowable soft robot that samples stomach fluid and measures acidity in real time. The robot has no battery, chip, nor any other electronics. Health care workers can move it with a handheld magnet, while it glides through the stomach like a penguin on its belly. The researchers published their work May 8 in Science Advances.

medical.title: Ozempic delivers major weight loss in adults over 65, study finds
medical.summary: A major new analysis suggests semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) works remarkably well in adults over 65, helping many lose substantial amounts of weight while improving heart and metabolic health. Participants taking the drug lost over 15% of their body weight on average — far more than those receiving placebo treatment. Many also moved out of obesity categories entirely and reached healthier weight levels.

medical.title: This 800-year-old Chinese exercise helps lower blood pressure naturally
medical.summary: An ancient Chinese exercise routine may be just as powerful as a daily brisk walk for lowering blood pressure — without equipment, gyms, or intense workouts. In a major clinical trial, adults with stage 1 hypertension who practiced baduanjin, a gentle mind-body exercise combining slow movements, breathing, and meditation, saw meaningful drops in blood pressure within three months that lasted for an entire year.

science.title: Black holes don't live forever, but they might live long enough to look like white holes
science.summary: Black holes live forever, at least according to general relativity. Once material crosses a black hole's event horizon, it is trapped forever, until the last day of cosmic time. But we know that isn't true. General relativity is a classical model. It doesn't take into account the fuzzy, indeterminate nature of the quantum. We don't have a complete and consistent theory of quantum gravity, but we do have some understanding of quantum black holes.

science.title: Machine learning proves that graphene is hydrophobic
science.summary: For more than a decade, a fundamental mystery has surrounded graphene—the one-atom-thick "wonder material" known for its exceptional strength, conductivity, and transparency. Despite its seemingly simple structure, one basic question has remained unresolved: Does graphene attract water, or repel it?

science.title: The G-value paradox: Why similar genes can lead to very different brains
science.summary: Biologists have long puzzled over why organisms with similar numbers of protein-coding genes can differ so dramatically in nervous system complexity. New research points to a potential link between the expanding diversity of RNA-binding proteins, which shape how genetic instructions are processed, and greater brain sophistication.

science.title: An everyday sweetener offers a surprisingly powerful engine for transparent, stretchable electronics
science.summary: Professor Kyungwho Choi's team of the School of Mechanical Engineering at Sungkyunkwan University, in collaboration with Professor Jinsoo Kim's team in the Department of Chemical Engineering at Kyung Hee University, have proposed a strategy that simultaneously overcomes the limitations of conventional hydrogel-based triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs)—namely low output performance, poor mechanical strength, and insufficient transparency—by utilizing biomimetic stevia.

science.title: Where scientists watch the forest breathe, findings uproot how people think about forest-atmosphere interactions
science.summary: Photosynthesis is the oldest carbon-capture technology on Earth. For eons, plants have pulled carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and locked carbon—the building block of life on our planet—into their bodies and roots.

science.title: Earth's first continents may trace back to subduction 3.5 billion years ago
science.summary: An international team of researchers' analysis of minerals from the Pilbara region of Western Australia has given new insight into how ancient continents on Earth formed as far back as 3.5 billion years ago. Professor Tony Kemp, from The University of Western Australia's School of Earth and Oceans, was a co-author of the study published in Science Advances, which was led by researchers at Nanjing University in China.

science.title: 'Natural', 'vegan', 'eco-friendly': Australia's food sustainability claims lack regulation
science.summary: Nearly 4 in 10 packaged food products on Australian supermarket shelves carry a sustainability claim but new research shows many of those claims are vague, unverified, and potentially misleading.

science.title: 'Elegant triangle' experiment suggests quantum internet may be closer than we think
science.summary: For more than 60 years, Bell's theorem has been the gold standard for demonstrating that quantum mechanics defies the rules of classical physics. Now, an international team of researchers, including Constructor University Professor Dr. Nicolas Gisin, has extended this principle to new limits, using an "elegant triangle" to reveal new forms of quantum nonlocality that specifically emerge in multi-node quantum networks.

science.title: Industrial fishing has been depleting midwater fish for decades, new study finds
science.summary: A new study led by scientists at Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution finds that industrial-scale fishing has been removing substantial biomass from the ocean's "twilight zone" for decades, challenging the common assumption that this vast midwater ecosystem remains largely unexploited. The work is published in the journal Global Change Biology.

science.title: How social science helps keep bugs off corn
science.summary: A new study suggests broad systemic problems play a significant role in disincentivizing corn growers from planting the refuge corn needed to keep pests from becoming resistant to the insect-killing traits in Bt corn. And social science can play a role in addressing this challenge. The paper, "Moving beyond grower compliance: why Bt corn resistance management depends on system-level coordination," is published in the Journal of Economic Entomology.

science.title: The National Science Board purge, explained
science.summary: Amid the many attention-grabbing headlines of 2026, there is a recent one that may have flown under the radar but shouldn't have. On April 24, the White House dismissed the entire 22-person board that oversees the National Science Foundation. The NSF is an independent federal agency that supports science and engineering in all 50 states and U.S. territories.

science.title: NASA’s Psyche probe is about to slingshot around Mars at 12,000 mph
science.summary: NASA’s Psyche spacecraft is about to pull off a dramatic close flyby of Mars, skimming just 2,800 miles above the planet to get a powerful gravitational boost on its journey to the mysterious metal-rich asteroid Psyche. The maneuver will save propellant while giving mission scientists a rare chance to test and calibrate the spacecraft’s instruments using Mars as a target. As Psyche approaches from the planet’s dark side, it’s expected to capture striking crescent views of Mars, search for faint dust rings around the planet, and even gather magnetic and cosmic ray data during the encounter.

science.title: NASA’s Curiosity rover accidentally pulled a rock out of Mars
science.summary: NASA’s Curiosity rover had an unexpectedly stubborn Mars souvenir after drilling into a rock nicknamed “Atacama” — the entire chunk ripped loose from the ground and stayed stuck to the rover’s drill. Engineers watched as Curiosity shook, vibrated, tilted, and spun the drill over several days in an effort to free the rock, while cameras captured the strange scene on the Red Planet.

science.title: Scientists say Dante’s Inferno described an asteroid impact 500 years before modern science
science.summary: Dante’s Inferno may have been far more than a religious epic. New research argues that the 14th-century poet essentially imagined a catastrophic asteroid impact centuries before modern science understood meteors. In this interpretation, Satan crashes into Earth like a giant cosmic object, blasting through the Southern Hemisphere and reshaping the planet itself — carving out the circles of Hell while forcing up Mount Purgatory on the opposite side of the globe.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: HONOR Talents Next Gen creators și-a deschis porțile la Grivița Creative Corner, într-o expoziție deschisă publicului până pe 15 mai București, 11 mai 2026 – HONOR a premiat, în cadrul… Articolul România se remarcă în competiția globală HONOR Talents: NextGen Creators premiază noua generație de artiști și designeri la București apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Pe 6 iunie, la Roaba de Cultură din București, are loc cea de-a 6-a ediție a Târgului de Jucării 2nd Life organizat de Asociația Green Revolution, un eveniment dedicat copiilor… Articolul Pe 6 iunie, copiii devin mici antreprenori la Târgul de Jucării 2nd Life! apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Authentic Talks – Ediția a VI-a: o seară despre puterea conflictului și gândirea pe cont propriu în era AI
mix_items.summary: Asociația pentru Dezvoltare și Învățare Experiențială (A.D.I.E.) organizează cea de-a șasea ediție a evenimentului Authentic Talks, un concept dedicat învățării experiențiale, dialogului autentic și conectării între oameni care doresc să… Articolul Authentic Talks – Ediția a VI-a: o seară despre puterea conflictului și gândirea pe cont propriu în era AI apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: TIFF.25: filmele celor două competiții internaționale
mix_items.summary: Cineaști aflați la primul sau al doilea lungmetraj din carieră concurează pentru trofeele competițiilor internaționale ale celei de-a 25-a ediții a Festivalului Internațional de Film Transilvania (Cluj-Napoca, 12-21 iunie 2026). 12… Articolul TIFF.25: filmele celor două competiții internaționale apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Fanii lui Georgescu știu de unde are acesta milioane pentru donații: din salariul de la Real Madrid
mix_items.summary: Simpatizanții lui Călin Georgescu râd de cei care neagă că gurul lor ar fi donat 4 milioane de dolari din banii personali orfanilor din România. Argumentele aduse de aceștia sunt considerate de-a dreptul ridicole. „Cum poți să spui că Domnul Georgescu nu avea acei bani, cât de ticălos să fii să susții așa ceva?”, a […] The post Fanii lui Georgescu știu de unde are acesta milioane pentru donații: din salariul de la Real Madrid appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Un Turn Neuroendoscopic Karl Storz 4K va fi donat Secției de Neurochirurgie 3 a Spitalului Clinic de Urgență Bagdasar-Arseni
mix_items.summary: ASOCIAȚIA ECOVALORIS ȘI AJ BRAND ANUNȚĂ FINANȚAREA INTEGRALĂ A PRIMEI FAZE A CAMPANIEI „PRECIZIE PENTRU VIAȚĂ, TEHNOLOGIE PENTRU NEUROCHIRURGIE” Un Turn Neuroendoscopic Karl Storz 4K va fi donat Secției de Neurochirurgie… Articolul Un Turn Neuroendoscopic Karl Storz 4K va fi donat Secției de Neurochirurgie 3 a Spitalului Clinic de Urgență Bagdasar-Arseni apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Turneul Ansamblurilor de Percuție ajunge la București
mix_items.summary: Pe 19 mai, de la ora 19:00, Zoli Tóth și Programul Național Cantus Mundi invită publicul la Concertul extraordinar de percuție T.A.P. 2026, găzduit de Centrul Cultural ARCUB din București… Articolul Turneul Ansamblurilor de Percuție ajunge la București apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Băluţă anunţă finalizarea primei etape la Planşeul Unirii: Zona Hanului lui Manuc intră în teste. Ciucu: lucrările sunt în grafic
mix_items.summary: Primarul Sectorului 4 Daniel Băluţă a anunţat, luni, că lucrările la prima etapă a refacerii Planşeului Unirii, în zona Hanului lui Manuc, au fost finalizate, iar sectorul va fi supus testelor realizate de specialiştii Universităţii Tehnice de Construcţii Bucureşti. și primarul Capitalei Ciprian Ciucu a declarat că investiţia este una de mare amploare şi a apreciat că lucrările sunt în graficul stabilit.

mix_items.title: 😂 Kelemen i-a adus lui Grindeanu desertul lui preferat: kürvős kalács
mix_items.summary: Un moment emoţionant a avut loc în această dimineaţă la consultările între partide! Ca să-şi arate disponibilitatea de a participa la orice fel de coaliţie, liderul UDMR Kelemen Hunor i-a dăruit şefului PSD Sorin Grindeanu desertul său preferat: un kürvős kalács. „Nimic nu e mai reprezentativ pentru spiritul UDMR decât un kürvős kalács proaspăt, cald, […] The post Kelemen i-a adus lui Grindeanu desertul lui preferat: kürvős kalács appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: FLASH SALE: 70% reducere la biletele pentru copii la Wonder Family Fest 2026, doar de Ziua Familiei (15 mai)
mix_items.summary: De Ziua Familiei, sărbătorită pe data de 15 mai, organizatorii celui mai mare festival pentru familii din România vin cu un cadou special: biletele și abonamentele de acces destinate copiilor au 70% reducere*, în ediție limitată de 3000 bucăți. Prețurile pornesc de la 147 de lei pentru biletele de o zi și 297 de lei pentru abonamentele cu acces general (pentru toate cele trei zile de festival).

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: 😂 O să fie WOW! După Zorba la greci, Metallica învaţă Dansul Pinguinului pentru concertul din România
mix_items.summary: După ce şi-au încântat fanii din Atena cu un fragment din „Zorba”, americanii de la Metallica sunt hotărâţi să se autodepăşească la concertul de pe Arena Naţională din Bucureşti! Aceştia au studiat ore întregi de înregistrări de la nunţi pentru a găsi un echivalent românesc pentru Zorba, şi se pare că l-au găsit: îndrăgitul Dans […] The post O să fie WOW! După Zorba la greci, Metallica învaţă Dansul Pinguinului pentru concertul din România appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Explicații pentru saltul „ratat” cu parașuta. Greșeala făcută de sportivul care a sărit pentru prima dată de la 1.200 de metri
mix_items.summary: Imagini cu ceea ce pare a fi un salt ratat cu parașuta și o aterizare norocoasă au creat senzație pe internet. Incidentul a avut loc în apropiere de Craiova, iar protagonistul este un elev al aeroclubului local. Acesta a avut probleme cu parașuta principală și a deschis-o pe cea de rezervă. Aterizarea a fost făcută în siguranță, a spus la Digi24 directorul general al Aeroclubului României, George Rotaru.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Dietary changes in older people can improve 'biological age'
mix_items.summary: Older Australians who reduce either dietary fat or animal-based protein show signs of reduced biological age, new research from the University of Sydney shows. Published in Aging Cell, the findings revealed 65- to 75-year-olds subjected to dietary intervention for just four weeks showed a reduction in "biological age" based on their biomarker profile.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Black holes don't live forever, but they might live long enough to look like white holes
mix_items.summary: Black holes live forever, at least according to general relativity. Once material crosses a black hole's event horizon, it is trapped forever, until the last day of cosmic time. But we know that isn't true. General relativity is a classical model. It doesn't take into account the fuzzy, indeterminate nature of the quantum. We don't have a complete and consistent theory of quantum gravity, but we do have some understanding of quantum black holes.

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Machine learning proves that graphene is hydrophobic
mix_items.summary: For more than a decade, a fundamental mystery has surrounded graphene—the one-atom-thick "wonder material" known for its exceptional strength, conductivity, and transparency. Despite its seemingly simple structure, one basic question has remained unresolved: Does graphene attract water, or repel it?

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: The G-value paradox: Why similar genes can lead to very different brains
mix_items.summary: Biologists have long puzzled over why organisms with similar numbers of protein-coding genes can differ so dramatically in nervous system complexity. New research points to a potential link between the expanding diversity of RNA-binding proteins, which shape how genetic instructions are processed, and greater brain sophistication.

mix_items.title: Annual frailty screening recommended for the over 65s
mix_items.summary: A new framework to prevent and manage frailty in older Australians has been developed in a national project led by University of Queensland researchers. The Australian Consensus Statements are recommendations designed in consultation with health care professionals, older adults with lived experience of frailty, and caregivers across the country.

mix_items.title: An everyday sweetener offers a surprisingly powerful engine for transparent, stretchable electronics
mix_items.summary: Professor Kyungwho Choi's team of the School of Mechanical Engineering at Sungkyunkwan University, in collaboration with Professor Jinsoo Kim's team in the Department of Chemical Engineering at Kyung Hee University, have proposed a strategy that simultaneously overcomes the limitations of conventional hydrogel-based triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs)—namely low output performance, poor mechanical strength, and insufficient transparency—by utilizing biomimetic stevia.

mix_items.title: A common cholesterol drug may weaken ovarian cancer's hidden shield
mix_items.summary: Ascites—the buildup of liquid in the belly—may be doing more than causing discomfort. A Duke University School of Medicine study finds this fluid helps cancer cells survive and spread—and that a decades-old cholesterol drug may be able to disrupt that protection.

mix_items.title: Where scientists watch the forest breathe, findings uproot how people think about forest-atmosphere interactions
mix_items.summary: Photosynthesis is the oldest carbon-capture technology on Earth. For eons, plants have pulled carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and locked carbon—the building block of life on our planet—into their bodies and roots.

mix_items.title: Earth's first continents may trace back to subduction 3.5 billion years ago
mix_items.summary: An international team of researchers' analysis of minerals from the Pilbara region of Western Australia has given new insight into how ancient continents on Earth formed as far back as 3.5 billion years ago. Professor Tony Kemp, from The University of Western Australia's School of Earth and Oceans, was a co-author of the study published in Science Advances, which was led by researchers at Nanjing University in China.

mix_items.title: Depression may be overdiagnosed in patients with ovarian cancer
mix_items.summary: People with ovarian cancer could be being overdiagnosed with depression due to the physical symptoms they experience as part of the cancer, according to international researchers. In addition to causing mental symptoms such as sadness and despair, depression can cause physical sensations including fatigue, headaches, back pain, gastrointestinal issues, and sleep problems.

mix_items.title: Brain-controlled hearing system isolates one speaker in noisy settings, first human tests show
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Columbia University's Zuckerman Institute have the first direct evidence from human studies that brain-controlled hearing technology can help people single out a voice in a crowd. These early findings suggest that researchers may one day develop a hearing augmentation device that can, among other feats, overcome the problems that conventional hearing aids have with noisy surroundings.

mix_items.title: 'Natural', 'vegan', 'eco-friendly': Australia's food sustainability claims lack regulation
mix_items.summary: Nearly 4 in 10 packaged food products on Australian supermarket shelves carry a sustainability claim but new research shows many of those claims are vague, unverified, and potentially misleading.

mix_items.title: 'Elegant triangle' experiment suggests quantum internet may be closer than we think
mix_items.summary: For more than 60 years, Bell's theorem has been the gold standard for demonstrating that quantum mechanics defies the rules of classical physics. Now, an international team of researchers, including Constructor University Professor Dr. Nicolas Gisin, has extended this principle to new limits, using an "elegant triangle" to reveal new forms of quantum nonlocality that specifically emerge in multi-node quantum networks.

mix_items.title: Urine test could flag bipolar, ADHD and anorexia years earlier
mix_items.summary: New research suggests a simple urine test could help spot conditions including bipolar disorder, ADHD and anorexia much sooner, easing pressure on health services where diagnoses can currently take months—even years.

mix_items.title: In older adults, adding whey protein doesn't make more muscle
mix_items.summary: Packing in the protein is all the rage. From cereal to pasta to nacho chips and more, food manufacturers are trying to get as much of the stuff into their products as possible, and one of the benefits they tout is that protein increases muscle mass and strength.

mix_items.title: Q&A: Expert explains why tick bites are sending people to the ER in droves
mix_items.summary: Early-season tick bites have been driving more people to emergency rooms since 2017, and Virginia, along with several other northeastern states, is in the bull's-eye.

mix_items.title: Industrial fishing has been depleting midwater fish for decades, new study finds
mix_items.summary: A new study led by scientists at Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution finds that industrial-scale fishing has been removing substantial biomass from the ocean's "twilight zone" for decades, challenging the common assumption that this vast midwater ecosystem remains largely unexploited. The work is published in the journal Global Change Biology.

mix_items.title: Attention to sex differences, relapse needed in early-stage studies of medication to treat alcohol use disorder
mix_items.summary: Early-stage studies of medications to treat high-risk alcohol use may be missing opportunities to identify potential therapeutic effects in certain populations, including women and people at risk for relapse, according to a systematic review published in Alcohol: Clinical & Experimental Research.

mix_items.title: Slow-dividing breast cancer cells may explain relapses decades after treatment
mix_items.summary: A new study by the Garvan Institute of Medical Research has uncovered a hidden mechanism explaining why breast cancer can return many years after successful treatment. Published in Nature Communications, the research reveals rogue cells that change their programming to allow them to divide at a remarkably slow pace, meaning they could form microscopic tumors that silently tick away in distant organs, evading detection for decades.

mix_items.title: Improving relationship quality may support recovery from alcohol use disorder
mix_items.summary: For people trying to reduce their heavy drinking, the quality of their interpersonal relationships early in their recovery process may contribute to their success. A study published in Alcohol: Clinical & Experimental Research found that those who were successful in achieving remission from alcohol use disorder had reduced negative interactions with their family and social network in the first month after they began their recovery process. The study suggests that training family and friends how to positively engage with people trying to reduce their drinking may improve their chances of success.

mix_items.title: How social science helps keep bugs off corn
mix_items.summary: A new study suggests broad systemic problems play a significant role in disincentivizing corn growers from planting the refuge corn needed to keep pests from becoming resistant to the insect-killing traits in Bt corn. And social science can play a role in addressing this challenge. The paper, "Moving beyond grower compliance: why Bt corn resistance management depends on system-level coordination," is published in the Journal of Economic Entomology.

mix_items.title: The National Science Board purge, explained
mix_items.summary: Amid the many attention-grabbing headlines of 2026, there is a recent one that may have flown under the radar but shouldn't have. On April 24, the White House dismissed the entire 22-person board that oversees the National Science Foundation. The NSF is an independent federal agency that supports science and engineering in all 50 states and U.S. territories.

mix_items.title: Alcohol use disorder and obesity may cause metabolic alterations that raise the risk of relapse, study suggests
mix_items.summary: Disrupted metabolic function in people with alcohol use disorder (AUD) may be amplified in the context of obesity, contributing to intensified alcohol cravings, according to a preliminary study that supports ongoing investigation of GLP-1 agonists as a potential AUD treatment.

mix_items.title: Laser-induced acoustic imaging maps hidden nerves and vessels during robot surgery
mix_items.summary: Surgery is a complicated endeavor. Even a successful surgery can lead to complications, and even the best surgeons sometimes have unsuccessful surgeries. A surgeon must rely on visual cues and their own experience to avoid hitting a nerve or a blood vessel, mistakes that can turn a simple surgery into a much more challenging one.

mix_items.title: The robotic penguin that makes endoscopy optional
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the TechMed Center of the University of Twente have built a swallowable soft robot that samples stomach fluid and measures acidity in real time. The robot has no battery, chip, nor any other electronics. Health care workers can move it with a handheld magnet, while it glides through the stomach like a penguin on its belly. The researchers published their work May 8 in Science Advances.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Psyche probe is about to slingshot around Mars at 12,000 mph
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Psyche spacecraft is about to pull off a dramatic close flyby of Mars, skimming just 2,800 miles above the planet to get a powerful gravitational boost on its journey to the mysterious metal-rich asteroid Psyche. The maneuver will save propellant while giving mission scientists a rare chance to test and calibrate the spacecraft’s instruments using Mars as a target. As Psyche approaches from the planet’s dark side, it’s expected to capture striking crescent views of Mars, search for faint dust rings around the planet, and even gather magnetic and cosmic ray data during the encounter.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Curiosity rover accidentally pulled a rock out of Mars
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Curiosity rover had an unexpectedly stubborn Mars souvenir after drilling into a rock nicknamed “Atacama” — the entire chunk ripped loose from the ground and stayed stuck to the rover’s drill. Engineers watched as Curiosity shook, vibrated, tilted, and spun the drill over several days in an effort to free the rock, while cameras captured the strange scene on the Red Planet.

mix_items.title: Scientists say Dante’s Inferno described an asteroid impact 500 years before modern science
mix_items.summary: Dante’s Inferno may have been far more than a religious epic. New research argues that the 14th-century poet essentially imagined a catastrophic asteroid impact centuries before modern science understood meteors. In this interpretation, Satan crashes into Earth like a giant cosmic object, blasting through the Southern Hemisphere and reshaping the planet itself — carving out the circles of Hell while forcing up Mount Purgatory on the opposite side of the globe.

mix_items.title: Ozempic delivers major weight loss in adults over 65, study finds
mix_items.summary: A major new analysis suggests semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) works remarkably well in adults over 65, helping many lose substantial amounts of weight while improving heart and metabolic health. Participants taking the drug lost over 15% of their body weight on average — far more than those receiving placebo treatment. Many also moved out of obesity categories entirely and reached healthier weight levels.

mix_items.title: This 800-year-old Chinese exercise helps lower blood pressure naturally
mix_items.summary: An ancient Chinese exercise routine may be just as powerful as a daily brisk walk for lowering blood pressure — without equipment, gyms, or intense workouts. In a major clinical trial, adults with stage 1 hypertension who practiced baduanjin, a gentle mind-body exercise combining slow movements, breathing, and meditation, saw meaningful drops in blood pressure within three months that lasted for an entire year.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am pus viata in ordine. Acum nu mai gasesc nimic.

satire_ro.title: Bucureştean, furios că i-au pus pătrunjel în şaorma: „Pentru asta am alergat ieri la maraton?”
